card.intuition: Expand palindromes around every center in O(n) by reusing mirror-symmetry: if center c has palindrome radius r, every center inside that radius has a known minimum expansion.
card.analogy: You've already measured how far a spotlight shines from the middle of a stage. Any performer standing inside that spotlight's edge has a pre-known minimum visibility range — you only need to re-check if they might be visible even further.
card.time_complexity: O(n) — each character is the center of an expansion at most once total
when_to_use: Longest palindromic substring in O(n) — beats the O(n²) expand-around-center approach
Counting all palindromic substrings in O(n)
When palindrome queries need to be answered online after O(n) preprocessing
gotchas: Transform s into '#a#b#a#' (insert separators) to unify odd and even-length palindromes — otherwise you need two separate cases
The radius p[i] in the transformed string equals the length of the palindrome in the original: len = p[i] (after transformation, subtract 1 for the separator)
When i > right boundary of the current widest palindrome, you must expand from scratch — the mirror trick only applies inside the boundary

exercise.prompt: Complete the expand-around-center helper (O(n²) approach) that Manacher's improves upon. Return the palindrome length.
exercise.starter_code:
def longest_palindrome(s):
    def expand(l, r):
        """Expand outward while s[l] == s[r]. Return palindrome length."""
        while l >= 0 and r < len(s) and s[l] == s[r]:
            l -= 1
            r += 1
        # After loop: l and r are one step OUTSIDE the palindrome
        return ___________

    best = 1
    for i in range(len(s)):
        odd  = expand(i, i)        # odd-length center
        even = expand(i, i + 1)    # even-length center
        best = ___________
    return best
exercise.solution:
return r - l - 1          # r and l overshot by 1 each: length = r - l - 1
best = max(best, odd, even)
hints: After the while loop, l and r have gone one step too far — the actual palindrome runs from l+1 to r-1, so its length is (r-1) - (l+1) + 1 = r - l - 1
Check both odd (single center) and even (two-character center) expansions, then take the maximum of all three values
exercise.test_code:
assert longest_palindrome('babad')   == 3, '"bab" or "aba" length 3'
assert longest_palindrome('cbbd')    == 2, '"bb" length 2'
assert longest_palindrome('a')       == 1, 'Single char'
assert longest_palindrome('racecar') == 7, 'Whole string is palindrome'
assert longest_palindrome('abcba')   == 5, 'Whole string'
assert longest_palindrome('aacecaaa') == 7, '"aacecaa" length 7'
print('PASS')
